title: DP & RL
text:
Method for solving Markov Decision Processes (MDPs).
DP assumes:

Model-based (known MDP): transition probabilities and rewards are given
The Markov property

DP uses the Bellman equation to iteratively update value functions.
Goal:

Compute the optimal value function
Derive the optimal policy




Value-based approach: Directly update value functions → Value Iteration
Policy-based approach: Evaluate and improve policies → Policy Iteration





Based on Bellman Optimality Equation:
\[v_*(s) = \max_a \sum_{s',r} p(s',r|s,a)[r + \gamma v_*(s')]\]


\[V_{k+1}(s) \leftarrow \max_a \sum_{s',r} p(s',r|s,a)[r + \gamma V_k(s')]\]
Initialize → Update → Compute the Optimal Policy

Initialize \(V_0(s) = 0\) for all states (or arbitrarily)
Iteratively update \(V_{k+1}(s)\) from \(V_k(s')\) (full backup) until convergence

Synchronous: Update at once after calculate \(V_{k+1}\) for all \(s\)
Asynchronous: Update immediately after calculate \(V_{k+1}\)

Extract the optimal policy with one-step lookahead after convergence:

\[\pi^*(s) = \arg\max_a \sum_{s',r} p(s',r|s,a)[r + \gamma V^*(s')]\]
Convergence: \(|V_{k+1}(s) - V_k(s)| < \epsilon\) for all \(s\)




Policy often converges long before the values converge — Repeat unnecessary iterations while the value converges below \(\epsilon\), even if the policy is already optimal.
Slow: \(O(S^2 A)\) per iteration, needs many iterations to converge.






\begin{algorithm} \caption{Value Iteration for estimating $\pi \approx \pi_*$} \begin{algorithmic} \State $\mathbf{Hyperparameter}$: small threshold $\epsilon > 0$ for convergence check \State Initialize $V(s)$ arbitrarily for all $s\in \mathcal{S}$, except $V(\text{terminal}) = 0$ \State \Repeat \State $\Delta\gets0$ \For{each $s\in\mathcal{S}$} \State $v\gets V(s)$ \State $V(s)\gets\max_a\sum+{s',r} p(s',r|s,a) [r+\gamma V(s')]$ \State $\Delta\gets\max(\Delta,|v-V(s)|)$ \EndFor \Until{$\Delta<\epsilon$} \State \State $\mathbf{Output}$: deterministic policy $\pi\approx\pi^*$ such that \State $\pi(s) = \arg\max_a \sum_{s',r} p(s',r|s,a) [r + \gamma V(s')]$ \end{algorithmic} \end{algorithm}






Repeats Policy Evaluation and Policy Improvement until convergence, and if the policy does not change, exit immediately.
\[\pi_0 \xrightarrow{PE} V^{\pi_0} \xrightarrow{PI} \pi_1 \xrightarrow{PE} V^{\pi_1} \xrightarrow{PI} \cdots \to \pi_* \to V^*\]


Compute \(V^\pi\) from the deterministic policy \(\pi\):
\[V_{k+1}(s) \leftarrow \sum_{s',r} p(s',r|s,\pi(s))[r + \gamma V_k(s')]\]

Fixed action \(\rightarrow\) \(O(S^2)\) / iteration
Update until \(|V_{k+1}(s) - V_k(s)| < \epsilon\)




Improve policy \(\pi\) to \(\pi'\) using a greedy policy based on \(V^\pi\):
\[\pi'(s) = \arg\max_a \sum_{s',r} p(s',r|s,a)[r + \gamma V^\pi(s')] = \arg\max_a Q^\pi(s,a)\]
Since \(Q^\pi(s, \pi'(s)) \geq V^\pi(s) = \sum_a \pi(a|s) Q^\pi(s,a)\), always either:

\(\pi'\) is strictly better than \(\pi\), or
\(\pi'\) is optimal when \(\pi = \pi'\)

→ Policy Improvement Theorem









ImportantPolicy Improvement Theorem



Let \(\pi\) and \(\pi'\) be two policies. If \(Q^\pi(s, \pi'(s)) \geq V^\pi(s)\) for all \(s \in S\), then \(V^{\pi'}(s) \geq V^\pi(s)\) for all \(s \in S\). This implies that \(\pi'\) is a better policy than \(\pi\).


Proof:
\[
\begin{aligned}
v_\pi(s) &\leq q_\pi(s, \pi'(s)) \\
&= \mathbb{E}[R_{t+1} + \gamma v_\pi(S_{t+1}) \mid S_t=s, A_t=\pi'(s)] \\
&= \mathbb{E}_{\pi'}[R_{t+1} + \gamma v_\pi(S_{t+1}) \mid S_t=s] \\
&\leq \mathbb{E}_{\pi'}[R_{t+1} + \gamma q_\pi(S_{t+1}, \pi'(S_{t+1})) \mid S_t=s] \\
&= \mathbb{E}_{\pi'}[R_{t+1} + \gamma \mathbb{E}_{\pi'}[R_{t+2} + \gamma v_\pi(S_{t+2}) \mid S_{t+1}, A_{t+1}=\pi'(S_{t+1})] \mid S_t=s] \\
&= \mathbb{E}_{\pi'}[R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^2 v_\pi(S_{t+2}) \mid S_t=s] \\
&\leq \mathbb{E}_{\pi'}[R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^2 R_{t+3} + \gamma^3 v_\pi(S_{t+3}) \mid S_t=s] \\
&\ \ \vdots \\
&\leq \mathbb{E}_{\pi'}[R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^2 R_{t+3} + \cdots \mid S_t=s] \\
&= v_{\pi'}(s)
\end{aligned}
\]



A finite MDP has finitely many policies \(\rightarrow\) Converge to optimal policy in finite iterations.
If \(\pi' = \pi\), \(v_\pi = v_{\pi'}\):
\[v_{\pi'}(s) = \max_a \sum_{s',r} p(s',r|s,a)[r + \gamma v_{\pi'}(s')]\]
\(\rightarrow\) Satisfy bellman optimality equation \(\rightarrow\) \(\pi'\) is optimal.





\begin{algorithm} \caption{Policy Iteration for estimating $\pi \approx \pi_*$} \begin{algorithmic} \State $\textbf{1. Initialization}$ \State $V(s) \in \mathbb{R}$ and $\pi(s) \in \mathcal{A}(s)$ arbitratily for all $s \in \mathcal{S}$ \State \State $\textbf{2. Policy Evaluation}$ \Repeat \State $\Delta \gets 0$ \For{each $s \in \mathcal{S}$} \State $v \gets V(s)$ \State $V(s) \gets \sum\limits_{s',r} p(s',r|s,\pi(s)) [r + \gamma V(s')]$ \State $\Delta \gets \max(\Delta, |v - V(s)|)$ \EndFor \Until{$\Delta<\epsilon$} \State \State $\textbf{3. Policy Improvement}$ \State $policy-stable$ $\gets$ true \For{each $s \in \mathcal{S}$} \State $old-action \gets \pi(s)$ \State $\pi(s) \gets \arg\max\limits_a \sum\limits_{s',r} p(s',r|s,a) [r + \gamma V(s')]$ \If{$old-action \neq \pi(s)$} \State $policy-stable \gets$ false \EndIf \EndFor \State \If{$policy-stable$} \State stop and return $V \approx v_*$ and $\pi\approx\pi_*$ \Else \State $\textbf{go to 2}$ \EndIf \end{algorithmic} \end{algorithm}










Value Iteration
Policy Iteration




Base Equation
Bellman Optimality
Bellman Expectation


O/iteration
\(O(S^2 A)\)
\(O(S^2)\) (PE)


Terminate Condition
\(\|V_{k+1}-V_k\| < \epsilon\)
\(\pi' = \pi\)


Extract Policy
Once after Converge
each step


Converge Speed
Slow
Fast



Reason why Policy Iteration is Fast: The amount of computation in one implementation step itself is the same as the max operation in Value Iteration. However, Terminate Condition is different - Value Iteration rotates until the value converges below \(\epsilon\), while Policy Iteration stops immediately if the policy does not change. Policy converges much earlier than value, so it benefits from the total number of iteration.



Finding an optimal policy by solving Bellman optimality equation requires:

Markov property
Accurate knowledge of environment dynamics (known MDP)
Enough space and time to do the computation

Dynamic Programming 특징:

Under model-based, each iteration updates every value function using full backup \(\rightarrow\) effective for medium-sized problems
Usually evaluate \(V(s)\) instead of \(Q(s,a)\) because \(|S| \ll |(S,A)|\)
For large problems, DP suffers from the curse of dimensionality

“The number of states grows exponentially with the number of state variables.”

\(\pi'\) is strictly better than \(\pi\), or
\(\pi'\) is optimal when \(\pi = \pi'\)

→ Policy Improvement Theorem









ImportantPolicy Improvement Theorem

Proof:
\[
\begin{aligned}
v_\pi(s) &\leq q_\pi(s, \pi'(s)) \\
&= \mathbb{E}[R_{t+1} + \gamma v_\pi(S_{t+1}) \mid S_t=s, A_t=\pi'(s)] \\
&= \mathbb{E}_{\pi'}[R_{t+1} + \gamma v_\pi(S_{t+1}) \mid S_t=s] \\
&\leq \mathbb{E}_{\pi'}[R_{t+1} + \gamma q_\pi(S_{t+1}, \pi'(S_{t+1})) \mid S_t=s] \\
&= \mathbb{E}_{\pi'}[R_{t+1} + \gamma \mathbb{E}_{\pi'}[R_{t+2} + \gamma v_\pi(S_{t+2}) \mid S_{t+1}, A_{t+1}=\pi'(S_{t+1})] \mid S_t=s] \\
&= \mathbb{E}_{\pi'}[R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^2 v_\pi(S_{t+2}) \mid S_t=s] \\
&\leq \mathbb{E}_{\pi'}[R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^2 R_{t+3} + \gamma^3 v_\pi(S_{t+3}) \mid S_t=s] \\
&\ \ \vdots \\
&\leq \mathbb{E}_{\pi'}[R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^2 R_{t+3} + \cdots \mid S_t=s] \\

\end{aligned}
\]



A finite MDP has finitely many policies \(\rightarrow\) Converge to optimal policy in finite iterations.
If \(\pi' = \pi\), \(v_\pi = v_{\pi'}\):
\[v_{\pi'}(s) = \max_a \sum_{s',r} p(s',r|s,a)[r + \gamma v_{\pi'}(s')]\]
\(\rightarrow\) Satisfy bellman optimality equation \(\rightarrow\) \(\pi'\) is optimal.

Initialization}$ \State $V(s) \in \mathbb{R}$ and $\pi(s) \in \mathcal{A}(s)$ arbitratily for all $s \in \mathcal{S}$ \State \State $\textbf{2. Policy Evaluation}$ \Repeat \State $\Delta \gets 0$ \For{each $s \in \mathcal{S}$} \State $v \gets V(s)$ \State $V(s) \gets \sum\limits_{s',r} p(s',r|s,\pi(s)) [r + \gamma V(s')]$ \State $\Delta \gets \max(\Delta, |v - V(s)|)$ \EndFor \Until{$\Delta<\epsilon$} \State \State $\textbf{3. title: DP & RL
text:
2. Reinforcement Learning

2.1 DP vs RL




Dynamic Programming
Reinforcement Learning




Setting
Model-based
Model-free


Backup
Full backup
Sample backup


Value
\(V(s)\)
\(Q(s,a)\)


Policy
Deterministic greedy
\(\epsilon\)-greedy


Update
Every state
sampled state


Experience
Simulated (from model)
Real (from environment)



Reason why DP uses \(V(s)\): We already know the transition probability \(p(s'|s,a)\), so we can derive the greedy policy using only \(V\):
\[\pi_*(s) = \arg\max_a \sum_{s',r} p(s',r|s,a)[r + \gamma V^*(s')]\]
Reason why RL uses \(Q(s,a)\): Since we don’t know the transition probability, we cannot use \(V\) alone to determine the policy. \(Q\) includes action information, allowing us to derive the policy without a model:
\[\pi'(s) = \arg\max_a Q^\pi(s,a)\]
RL Methods:

Monte Carlo (MC) method
Temporal Difference (TD) learning

SARSA
Q-learning




2.2 Generalized Policy Iteration (GPI)
Almost all RL methods can be described as GPI:
\[\pi_0 \to q_{\pi_0} \to \pi_1 \to q_{\pi_1} \to \cdots \to \pi_* \to q_*\]

Policy Evaluation (PE): Update value function to current policy
Policy Improvement (PI): Update policy in a greedy manner with respect to the current value function

If both PE and PI stabilize, Bellman optimality equation is satisfied \(\rightarrow\) optimal.


2.3 Monte Carlo Methods

MC Features

Repeated random sampling to compute numerical results
Tabular updating, model-free, no bootstrapping
Learns from entire trajectories of sampled episodes \(\rightarrow\) only apply to episodic tasks
Robust to violations of the Markov property (does not depend on successor value estimates)

\[\text{Value} = \text{average of returns } G_t \text{ of sampled episodes}\]
MC Policy Iteration GPI implements GPIs on an episode-by-episode basis:

Policy Evaluation: Estimate \(Q(s,a) = q_\pi(s,a)\)
Policy Improvement: \(\epsilon\)-greedy



MC Prediction (Policy Evaluation)
Goal: learn \(q_\pi\) from entire episodes of real experience under policy \(\pi\).
MC Policy Evaluation uses empirical mean return instead of expected return.
To estimate \(q_\pi(s,a)\), for each time step \(t\) when \((s,a)\) is visited:

Increment count: \(n(s,a) \gets n(s,a) + 1\)
Increment total return: \(S(s,a) \gets S(s,a) + G_t\)
Estimate mean return: \(Q(s,a) = S(s,a) / n(s,a)\)

As \(n(s,a) \to \infty\), \(Q(s,a) \to q_\pi(s,a)\) (law of large numbers)
First-Visit MC vs Every-Visit MC:

First-Visit: Update first visit to \((s,a)\) within an episode → Stable Convergence
Every-Visit: Update every visit to \((s,a)\) within an episode → Biased short returns mixed in, leading to unstable convergence
Running episodes infinitely makes both converge to the true value



Incremental MC Updates
\[\mu_k = \mu_{k-1} + \frac{1}{k}(x_k - \mu_{k-1})\]
\[Q(S_t, A_t) \gets Q(S_t, A_t) + \frac{1}{n(S_t, A_t)}[G_t - Q(S_t, A_t)]\]


Constant-\(\alpha\) MC
\[Q(S_t, A_t) \gets Q(S_t, A_t) + \alpha[G_t - Q(S_t, A_t)]\]
Develop recursively:
\[Q_n = \alpha G_n + (1-\alpha)\alpha G_{n-1} + (1-\alpha)^2 \alpha G_{n-2} + \cdots\]
The weight of the \(k\)-th past sample is \((1-\alpha)^k \alpha\) \(\rightarrow\) exponentially forgotten

\(\frac{1}{n}\) type: Equal average of all pasts
Constant-\(\alpha\): Exponentially forgotten \(\rightarrow\) important for non-stationary
If \(\alpha = 1\) most recent \(G_t\) only is reflected and previous values are completely discarded




2.4 Exploitation vs Exploration

Exploitation: Making the best decision by using already known information \(\rightarrow\) Refine our policy efficiently from al already promising regions
Exploration: Searching for new decisions by gathering more information \(\rightarrow\) Select random action with \(\epsilon\)-probability

To trade-off Exploitation and Exploration, use \(\epsilon\)-greedy policy.


2.5 MC Control (\(\epsilon\)-Greedy Policy Improvement)
\[\pi'(a|s) = \begin{cases} 1 - \epsilon + \epsilon/m & \text{if } a = \arg\max_{a'} Q^\pi(s,a') \\ \epsilon/m & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}\]

All actions are selected with non-zero probability \(\rightarrow\) guaranteed continuous exploration
In full backup DP, \(\pi'(s) = \arg\max_a Q^\pi(s,a)\) \(\rightarrow\) deterministic policy







Note\(\epsilon\)-Greedy Policy Improvement



For any \(\epsilon\)-greedy policy \(\pi\), \(\epsilon\)-greedy policy \(\pi'\) w.r.t. \(q_\pi\) is always improved: \[v_{\pi'}(s) \ge v_{\pi}(s)\]


Proof:
\[
\begin{aligned}
q_\pi(s,\pi'(s)) &= \sum_a \pi'(a|s) q_\pi(s,a) \\
&= \frac{\epsilon}{m}\sum_a q_\pi(s,a) + (1-\epsilon)\max_{a'} q_\pi(s,a') \\
&\geq \frac{\epsilon}{m}\sum_a q_\pi(s,a) + (1-\epsilon)\sum_a \underbrace{\frac{\pi(a|s)-\epsilon/m}{1-\epsilon}}_{\text{Prob. dist. with sum 1}} q_\pi(s,a) \\
&= \sum_a \pi(a|s) q_\pi(s,a) = v_\pi(s)
\end{aligned}
\]


2.6 GLIE (Greedy in the Limit with Infinite Exploration)
Both conditions must be satisfied simultaneously:
Condition 1 — Explored infinitely:
\[\lim_{k \to \infty} n_k(s,a) = \infty\]
Condition 2 — policy converges to greedy policy:
\[\lim_{k \to \infty} \pi_k(a|s) = 1 \quad \text{where } a = \arg\max_{a'} Q_k(s,a')\]
Set to \(\epsilon_k = \frac{1}{k}\) to satisfy both conditions simultaneously:
\[\pi_k(a^*|s) = 1 - \frac{1}{k} + \frac{1}{mk} \to 1\]
Why are both conditions necessary:







Condition
Problem




Condition 1 only
Continuously exploring \(\rightarrow\) cannot converge to greedy policy


Condition 2 only
Too early convergence to greedy \(\rightarrow\) unvisited \((s,a)\) pairs occur



Problems with fixed \(\epsilon\)
The probability of selecting the greedy action remains permanently below 1: \(1 - \epsilon + \epsilon/m < 1\) \(\rightarrow\) cannot converge to the true optimal policy
GLIE MC Control:

In the \(k\)-th episode, for each \((S_t, A_t)\):

\(n(S_t, A_t) \gets n(S_t, A_t) + 1\)
\(Q(S_t, A_t) \gets Q(S_t, A_t) + \frac{1}{n(S_t, A_t)}[G_t - Q(S_t, A_t)]\)

Improve policy with the new action-value function: \(\epsilon = \frac{1}{k}\), \(\pi \gets \epsilon\text{-greedy}(Q)\)

\(Q(s,a) \to q_*(s,a)\) (converge to optimal)

MC Method Pseudo Code


\begin{algorithm} \caption{Monte Carlo Method} \begin{algorithmic} \State $\textbf{Initialize}$ $Q(S,A)$, all $S\in\mathcal{S}$, $A\in\mathcal{A}(S)$, arbitrarily and $Q(\text{terminal},\cdot)=0$ \State $\hspace{2em}Returns(S,A)$ $\gets$ empty list \State $\hspace{2em}\pi \gets$ arbitrarily $\epsilon$-soft policy (non-empty probabilities) \State \Repeat \State (a) Generate an episode using $\pi$: $S_0, A_0, R_1, S_1, \ldots, S_{T-1}, A_{T-1}, R_T$ \State $\hspace{1.5em}G \gets 0$ \State (b) Repeat (for each step of episode), $t=T-1, T-2, \ldots, 0$: \State $\hspace{1.5em}G \gets \gamma G + R_{t+1}$ \State $\hspace{1.5em}$ Unless $(S_t, A_t)$ appears in $(S_0, A_0), \ldots, (S_{t-1}, A_{t-1})$: $\gets$ first-visit only \State $\hspace{3.5em}$ Append $G$ to $Returns(S_t, A_t)$ \State $\hspace{3.5em}$ $Q(S_t, A_t) \gets$ average($Returns(S_t, A_t)$) \State (c) For each $S_t$ in the episode: \State $\hspace{1.5em}A^* \gets \arg\max_a Q(S_t, a)$ \State $\hspace{1.5em}$ For all $a \in \mathcal{A}(S_t)$: \State $\hspace{1.5em}\pi(a|S_t) \gets \begin{cases} 1 - \epsilon + \frac{\epsilon}{|\mathcal{A}(S)|} & \text{if } a = A^* \\ \frac{\epsilon}{|\mathcal{A}(S)|} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$ \Until{forever (for each episode)} \end{algorithmic} \end{algorithm}

title: MDP & Bellman Equation
text:
Robotics & Autonomous Driving
Game AI
Finance & Trading
Recommendation System
Optimization System





State (\(S_t\)): State of Environment at time \(t\).
Action (\(A_t\)): Action of Agent based on \(S_t\).
Environment: Generate new state \(S_{t+1}\) and reward \(R_{t+1}\) based on agent’s action.
Reward (\(R_{t+1}\)): Scalar feedback indicating the effect of the action.

Environment: Generate new state \(S_{t+1}\) and reward \(R_{t+1}\) based on agent’s action.

title: MDP & Bellman Equation
text:
2. Markov Decision Process

2.1 Grid World
Deterministic grid world vs Stochastic grid world:

Deterministic: Output is fully determined by the present state and action.
Stochastic: Random outputs from same state and action.



2.2 Markov Property
Stochastic Process:

Discrete-time random process: \(S_0, S_1, \ldots, S_t, \ldots\)
Continuous-time random process: \(\{S_t \mid t \geq 0\}\)

Markov Property (memoryless property):
\[P(S_{t+1} = s' \mid S_t = s) = P(S_{t+1} = s' \mid S_0, S_1, \ldots, S_t = s)\]

Given the present state \(S_t = s\), the future state \(S_{t+1} = s\) does not depend on past states.

Markov Process \((S, P)\):

\(S\): Finite set of states
\(P\): State transition probability matrix, \(P_{ij} = P(s_j \mid s_i) = P(S_{t+1}=s_j \mid S_t=s_i)\)

Sum of entries in each row = 1




2.3 Markov Decision Process (MDP)
An MDP is a tuple \((S, A, P, R, \gamma)\).



Symbol
Meaning




\(S\)
State space


\(A\)
Action space


\(P^a_{ss'} = p(s' \mid s, a)\)
State transition probability


\(R\)
Reward function (\(R_s\), \(R_s^a\), \(R_{ss'}^a\))


\(\gamma \in [0, 1]\)
Discount factor




Model-based: known MDP (Given transition probability)
Model-free: unknown MDP (Ungiven transition probability)

MDP theory holds even if \(S\) and \(A\) is continuous.

title: MDP & Bellman Equation
text:
3. Reward & Policy

3.1 Reward

Reward \(R_t\): Scalar feedback
Goal of Agent: Maximize cumulative sum of rewards







NoteReward Hypothesis



All goals can be described by the maximization of the expected value of the cumulative sum of rewards.




3.2 Type of Reward
If we know known dynamics \(p(s', r \mid s, a)\):
State Transition Probability (marginalization over reward):
\[P_{ss'}^a = p(s' \mid s, a) = \sum_{r \in R} p(s', r \mid s, a)\]
Expected Reward for State-Action Pair:
\[R_s^a = r(s,a) = E[R_{t+1} \mid S_t=s, A_t=a] = \sum\limits_{r \in R} r \sum_{s' \in S} p(s', r \mid s, a)\]
Expected Reward for State-Action-Next State Triple:
\[
\begin{aligned}
R_{ss'}^a &= r(s,a,s') = E[R_{t+1} \mid S_t=s, A_t=a, S_{t+1}=s'] \\
&= \frac{\sum_{r \in R} r \cdot p(s', r \mid s, a)}{p(s' \mid s, a)}
\end{aligned}
\]


3.3 Return
\[G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \cdots = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}\]
Why are most MDPs discounted?:

Mathematically convenient (avoids infinite return)
Accounts for uncertainty (decay exponentially)
Immediate rewards are more valuable than delayed rewards
If all sequences terminate, an undiscounted return(\(\gamma=1\)) can be used



3.4 Policy

Stochastic policy: \(\pi(a \mid s) = P(A_t = a \mid S_t = s)\)
Deterministic policy: \(\pi(s) = a\)



Deterministic optimal policy \(\pi_*(s)\) exists in known MDP.
\(\epsilon\)-greedy policy (stochastic) is needed in unknown MDP:

\(1 - \epsilon\): Choose optimal action
\(\epsilon\): Choose random action




3.5 Summary of Notations

\(\pi\): Policy
\(\pi(a \mid s)\): Stochastic policy
\(\pi(s)\): Deterministic policy
\(v_\pi(s)\): State-value function
\(v_*(s)\): Optimal state-value function
\(q_\pi(s, a)\): Action-value function
\(q_*(s, a)\): Optimal action-value function

title: MDP & Bellman Equation
text:
4. Value Functions
When following a policy \(\pi\), value functions measure the goodness of each state \(s\) (or state-action pair \((s,a)\)), in terms of the expectation of returns \(G_t\).

4.1 State-Value Function
\[v_\pi(s) = E_\pi[G_t \mid S_t = s] = \sum_a \pi(a \mid s) q_\pi(s,a)\]


4.2 Action-Value Function
\[q_\pi(s, a) = E_\pi[G_t \mid S_t = s, A_t = a]\]


4.3 Advantage Function
\[A_\pi(s, a) = q_\pi(s, a) - v_\pi(s)\]

title: MDP & Bellman Equation
text:
5. Bellman Expectation Equation

5.1 Proof State-Value Function
\[
\begin{aligned}
v_\pi(s) &= E_\pi[G_t \mid S_t = s] \\
&= \sum_a E_\pi[G_t \mid S_t=s, A_t=a] \cdot P(A_t=a \mid S_t=s) \\
&= \sum_a \pi(a|s) E_\pi[R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1} \mid S_t=s, A_t=a] \\
&= \sum_a \pi(a|s) \sum_{s',r} p(s',r|s,a) \left[r + \gamma E_\pi[G_{t+1} \mid S_{t+1}=s']\right] \\
&= \sum_a \pi(a|s) \sum_{s',r} \left[r \cdot p(s',r|s,a) + \gamma E_\pi[G_{t+1}|S_{t+1}=s'] \cdot p(s',r|s,a)\right] \\
&= \sum_a \pi(a|s) \left[ R^a_s + \gamma \sum_{s'} P^a_{ss'} v_\pi(s') \right] \\
&= \sum_a \pi(a|s) E[R_{t+1} + \gamma v_\pi(S_{t+1}) \mid S_t=s, A_t=a] \\
&= E_\pi[R_{t+1} + \gamma v_\pi(S_{t+1}) \mid S_t = s]
\end{aligned}
\]


5.2 Precautions for induction
In the proof above, \(p(s', r| s, a)\) is marginalized in two directions.
\(r\) term — marginalization for \(s'\):
\[\sum_{s',r} r \cdot p(s',r|s,a) = \sum_r r \underbrace{\sum_{s'} p(s',r|s,a)}_{=p(r|s,a)} = E[R_{t+1} \mid s,a] = R^a_s\]
\(v_\pi(s')\) term — marginalization for \(r\):
\[\sum_{s',r} p(s',r|s,a) \cdot v_\pi(s') = \sum_{s'} \underbrace{\sum_r p(s',r|s,a)}_{=p(s'|s,a)=P^a_{ss'}} v_\pi(s')\]


5.3 Marking caution

\(r\): summation variable
\(R_{t+1}\): random variable
\(R^a_s\) = \(E[R_{t+1} \mid s,a]\)



5.4 Proof Action-Value Function
\[
\begin{aligned}
q_\pi(s,a) &= E_\pi[G_t \mid S_t=s, A_t=a] \\
&= \sum_{s',r} p(s',r|s,a)\left[r + \gamma E_\pi[G_{t+1} \mid S_{t+1}=s']\right] \\
&= \sum_{s',r} p(s',r|s,a)\left[r + \gamma \sum_{a'} \pi(a'|s') q_\pi(s',a')\right] \\
&= E[R_{t+1} + \gamma q_\pi(S_{t+1}, A_{t+1}) \mid S_t=s, A_t=a]
\end{aligned}
\]


5.5 Backup Diagram Relationship
\[
\begin{aligned}
v_\pi(s) &= \sum_a \pi(a|s) q_\pi(s,a)\\
q_\pi(s,a) &= R_s^a + \gamma \sum_{s'} P_{ss'}^a v_\pi(s')\\
v_\pi(s) &= \sum_a \pi(a|s) \left[ R_s^a + \gamma \sum_{s'} P_{ss'}^a v_\pi(s') \right]\\
q_\pi(s,a) &= R_s^a + \gamma \sum_{s'} P_{ss'}^a \sum_{a'} \pi(a'|s') q_\pi(s', a')
\end{aligned}
\]

title: MDP & Bellman Equation
text:
6. Bellman Optimality Equation

6.1 Optimal Value Functions
\[v_*(s) = \max_\pi v_\pi(s), \qquad q_*(s,a) = \max_\pi q_\pi(s,a)\]


6.2 Theorem (Optimal Policy Existence)






Note[Theorem] Any MDP satisfies the following:




There exists an optimal policy \(\pi_*\) such that \(\pi_* \ge \pi\) for all \(\pi\).
All optimal policies achieve the optimal state-value function: \(v_{\pi_*}(s) = v_*(s)\)
All optimal policies achieve the optimal action-value function: \(q_{\pi_*} (s,a) = q_*(s,a)\)





6.3 Finding an Optimal Policy
\[\pi_*(a \mid s) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } a = \arg\max_a q_*(s,a) \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}\]

There is always deterministic optimal policy for any MDP.
If we find \(q_*(s,a)\), we immediately have the policy: \(\pi_*(s) = \arg\max_a q_*(s,a)\)



6.4 Important formulas
\[v_*(s) = \max_a q_*(s,a) \quad \because v_\pi(s) = \sum_a \pi(a|s) q_\pi(s,a)\]
\[q_*(s,a) = r(s,a) + \gamma \sum_{s'} p(s'|s,a) v_*(s') = R_s^a + \gamma \sum_{s'} P^a_{ss'} v_*(s')\]
Important asymmetry:

\(v_*(s)\) can be obtained directly from \(q_*(s,a)\).
\(q_*(s,a)\) cannot be obtained directly from \(v_*(s)\).

Model-based setting: Need to know the transition probability \(p(s'|s,a)\)
Model-free setting: Directly compute \(Q(s,a)\)




6.5 Bellman Optimality Equation
\[v_*(s) = \max_a \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)\left[r + \gamma v_*(s')\right]\]
\[q_*(s,a) = R_s^a + \gamma \sum_{s'} P_{ss'}^a \max_{a'} q_*(s', a')\]
